question_frequency.rationale: The Pair asks a couple of logistical questions (language preference, one-off vs. series) but these are project-scoping questions, not didactic ones targeting teachable moments. No questions probe the user's understanding of inclusion-exclusion, the off-by-one in floor(999/k), or why LCM=15.

question_quality.rationale: The questions posed (language choice, harness scope) are about logistics rather than the crux of the problem. The genuinely interesting fork — brute-force vs. closed-form — was presented as a comparison table after the fact, not as a choice for the user to make.

scaffolding.rationale: The full mathematical solution including the answer 233,168 was revealed in Turn 1 before any user engagement, eliminating the opportunity to scaffold discovery. The subsequent build-out is logical but didactic progression is absent since the punchline came first.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and conversational, with clean back-and-forth on scoping questions and a tidy summary at the end. Flow is smooth, though it leans more toward collaborative project-setup than pedagogical dialogue.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code correctly produces 233,168 via both brute-force and closed-form, plus a working runner, template, and tsconfig — materially more deliverable than baseline's pure math answer, while still solving the original problem.

friction.rationale: Experiment took 3 turns / 138s vs. baseline's 1 turn / 14s, but produced a real TypeScript harness with two implementations rather than just an arithmetic answer. The extra length is roughly proportional to the additional artifacts delivered, though pedagogical payoff for the extra time is modest.
